configure-and-initialize-a-repository.label: Initialize the Community Site Repository
configure-and-initialize-a-repository.summary: Turn community-site into a repository on main and configure the supplied identity locally.

sketch0.title: An Ordinary Folder Becomes a Repository

sketch1.title: Repository Metadata Stays Behind the Scenes

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Initialize the Community Site Folder
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt: The terminal opens inside `community-site/`. It is an ordinary folder containing supplied project files. Type `git init -b main` and press Enter. Do not edit any project file.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Initialize the current folder and choose the initial branch in the same command.

help.concept: git init creates local repository metadata; `-b main` names the initial branch.
help.hint_1: The terminal is already inside community-site, so do not create or enter another folder.
help.hint_2: This step changes repository metadata only.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Community Site

Local events and resources.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions: The terminal opens inside `community-site/`. It is an ordinary folder containing supplied project files. Type `git init -b main` and press Enter. Do not edit any project file.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf -- community-site mkdir -p -- community-site mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s '# Community Site Local events and resources. ' > community-site/README.md mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s ' Community Site

Community Events

' > community-site/index.html mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s 'body { font-family: sans-serif; } footer { margin-top: 12px; } ' > community-site/styles.css mkdir -p -- community-site/notes printf %s 'Archive the spring event list. ' > community-site/notes/old-events.txt mkdir -p -- community-site/dist printf %s 'console.log('"'"'legacy preview'"'"'); ' > community-site/dist/legacy-preview.js

#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf -- community-site mkdir -p -- community-site mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s '# Community Site Local events and resources. ' > community-site/README.md mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s ' Community Site

Community Events

' > community-site/index.html mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s 'body { font-family: sans-serif; } footer { margin-top: 12px; } ' > community-site/styles.css mkdir -p -- community-site/notes printf %s 'Archive the spring event list. ' > community-site/notes/old-events.txt mkdir -p -- community-site/dist printf %s 'console.log('"'"'legacy preview'"'"'); ' > community-site/dist/legacy-preview.js

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Set the Local Author Name
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt: Inside `community-site/`, type `git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"` and press Enter. The `--local` scope keeps the value inside this repository.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Use repository-local configuration and the exact supplied name.

help.concept: A local identity applies only to the current repository.
help.hint_1: Keep the quotation marks around the name because it contains a space.
help.hint_2: Do not use --global in this course workspace.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf -- community-site mkdir -p -- community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s '# Community Site Local events and resources. ' > community-site/README.md mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s ' Community Site

Community Events

' > community-site/index.html mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s 'body { font-family: sans-serif; } footer { margin-top: 12px; } ' > community-site/styles.css mkdir -p -- community-site/notes printf %s 'Archive the spring event list. ' > community-site/notes/old-events.txt mkdir -p -- community-site/dist printf %s 'console.log('"'"'legacy preview'"'"'); ' > community-site/dist/legacy-preview.js

Community Events

' > community-site/index.html mkdir -p -- community-site printf %s 'body { font-family: sans-serif; } footer { margin-top: 12px; } ' > community-site/styles.css mkdir -p -- community-site/notes printf %s 'Archive the spring event list. ' > community-site/notes/old-events.txt mkdir -p -- community-site/dist printf %s 'console.log('"'"'legacy preview'"'"'); ' > community-site/dist/legacy-preview.js

Did you run `git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"`?

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Set the Local Author Email
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt: Inside `community-site/`, type `git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"` and press Enter. Then type `git config --local --list` and press Enter to verify both supplied values.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Store the exact email locally, then read the local configuration back.

help.concept: Git records an author identity with each commit; this course uses a safe repository-only identity.
help.hint_2: The verification command should show both user.name and user.email.

single-choice-1.title: Repository-Local Identity
single-choice-1.prompt: Why does this course use `git config --local` for the supplied author identity?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: It changes the identity only inside the current repository.
options.b: It deletes any existing project history.
options.c: It stages every file in the project.
options.d: It changes the contents of the project files.

single-choice-2.title: Repository Initialization
single-choice-2.prompt: What command is used to initialize a new Git repository?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order the First Repository Setup
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the setup steps in an order that can actually work.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Initialize the project folder as a repository

fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to set the local Git user name: `git config --local user.name [blank1]`
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.

sketch0.title: An Ordinary Folder Becomes a Repository
sketch0.bodyMarkdown: `community-site/` begins as an ordinary folder. `git init -b main` adds local repository metadata without changing the supplied project files. The terminal opens in the correct folder, so the important decision is to initialize the project root—not its parent and not a nested folder.

sketch1.title: Repository Metadata Stays Behind the Scenes
sketch1.bodyMarkdown: Git stores its internal records in `.git/`. You do not edit that directory in this course. Instead, commands such as `git status` ask Git to interpret the metadata safely.

sketch2.title: Identity Belongs to This Repository
sketch2.bodyMarkdown: Commits need an author name and email. `git config --local` stores the supplied course identity only in `community-site/`, avoiding machine-wide changes. This identity is setup for later commits, not a GitHub account.
